nutrients.name: Boron
nutrients.mobility: IMMOBILE in most crops (deficiency on YOUNG leaves, growing points, flowers, fruit)

overview.whyImportant: Boron is vital for cell-wall formation, pollen viability, flowering, fruit/seed set and sugar transport. It has the NARROWEST margin between deficiency and toxicity of any nutrient. Deficiency causes hollow stem/browning in cauliflower, fruit cracking, flower drop and poor seed set.

plantFunctions.roleInsidePlant: Cell-wall/membrane integrity, sugar transport, pollen-tube growth.
plantFunctions.vegetativeGrowth: Cell division at growing points; deficiency kills tips.
plantFunctions.flowering: CRITICAL — pollen viability, pollination, fertilisation; deficiency causes flower drop and blank set.
plantFunctions.yieldContribution: Large in crucifers, fruit/seed crops — governs set and quality.

whyDeficiencyHappens.why: B availability drops sharply above pH 7 — over-liming is a common trigger.
B leaches readily from acid sandy soils.
Low B reserves; leaches easily (B is mobile in soil).
B leaches quickly — among the most leachable nutrients.
Impaired uptake.
Leaches B below root zone.
Organic matter is a key B reservoir — low OM = low B.
B uptake needs moisture and transpiration; dry topsoil (where roots and B are) stops uptake — a very common cause.

whyDeficiencyHappens.condition: Nutrient imbalance / high Ca, K, N
whyDeficiencyHappens.why: High Ca (over-liming) and heavy K/N reduce B uptake.
Antagonise B absorption.
Immobile B needs continuous root uptake.
Slows uptake.
Impaired uptake (though some saline waters carry toxic B).
Not applying borax in crucifers/oilseeds; over-liming; ignoring B on sandy/leached soils.

symptoms.note: B IMMOBILE — deficiency on YOUNG leaves, growing points, FLOWERS and FRUIT. Signature: death of growing point, hollow/corky stems, poor set, cracking.
symptoms.early: Young leaves thick, brittle, distorted; growing point weakening.
symptoms.middle: Death of growing point, corky/cracked stems and petioles, flower drop, poor fruit set.
symptoms.severe: Hollow stem/browning (cauliflower), fruit cracking/corking, blank seed set, terminal dieback.
symptoms.youngLeaves: Thick, brittle, distorted, chlorotic tips; growing point dies.
symptoms.flower: Heavy flower drop, poor pollination, sterility.
symptoms.fruit: Cracking, corking, internal browning, poor set, deformed fruit.
symptoms.seed: Blank/poor seed set — major in seed crops.
symptoms.wholePlant: Healthy old leaves but dead growing tips, hollow stems, dropped flowers and cracked/hollow produce — top-down + reproductive damage.

cropWiseSymptoms.symptoms: White rolled young leaves, poor panicle/grain set on B-poor soils (uncommon).
Growing-point death, flower drop, poor pod set.
'Hollow heart'/internal darkening of kernel, poor pod fill.
Flower drop, poor fruit set, distorted young leaves, corky fruit.
Flower/fruit drop, corky/cracked fruit, growing-tip damage.
BROWNING of curd + HOLLOW/cracked stem + bitter curd — THE classic boron-deficiency crop.
Hollow/cracked stem, internal browning, poor heads.
Flower drop, mis-shapen/corky fruit, brittle young leaves.
Flower drop, fruit cracking/corking, distorted young leaves.
Flower drop, deformed pods, poor set.
Barren/poorly-filled tips, white-striped young leaves, poor kernel set.
Poor grain set, distorted young leaves on sandy soils.
Flower drop, poor pod set.
Flower/pod drop, poor set.
Young-leaf distortion, corky midrib, tip dieback on sandy/leached soils.

howToFix.soilApplicationDose: 4-5 kg/acre basal (crucifers/oilseeds); mix well for even spread
howToFix.bestCropStage: Basal + at pre-flowering/curd initiation; foliar at flowering
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Soil basal (even spread critical) or foliar at flowering/curding
howToFix.precautions: NARROW safety margin — do NOT exceed dose (toxicity easy); mix thoroughly to avoid hot spots; dissolve borax in warm water.

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Foliar/fertigation — highly soluble, good for flower-set correction
howToFix.precautions: Same narrow margin — stick to dose; ideal for foliar/fertigation.

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Convenient even B distribution with base fertiliser
howToFix.precautions: Ensures even low-dose B; avoids hot spots.

methodOfApplication.broadcast: Borax basal + incorporate — MUST mix thoroughly (with sand/FYM) to avoid toxic hot spots.
methodOfApplication.bandPlacement: Low-rate banding; risk of concentration — be careful.
methodOfApplication.foliarSpray: 0.1-0.2% borax/boric acid at flowering/curding — the safest, most targeted method.
methodOfApplication.dripFertigation: Solubor/boric acid at low dose via drip.

toxicity.whatHappens: B has the NARROWEST deficiency-toxicity range — slight over-dose is toxic.
toxicity.plantToxicitySymptoms: Yellowing then BROWN SCORCHING of OLDER-leaf margins and tips (B is mobile-to-margins in transpiration), leaf-edge burn, defoliation.
toxicity.qualityReduction: Leaf burn reduces photosynthesis and yield.
toxicity.correctionMethods: Stop all B; leach with irrigation on light soils; add organic matter; avoid high-B irrigation water; never re-apply without soil test.

prevention: Apply borax 4-5 kg/acre basal in crucifers/oilseeds (cauliflower, mustard) on B-poor soils.
NEVER exceed the dose — mix thoroughly to avoid hot spots.
Keep TOPSOIL MOIST (mulch/irrigation) — dry topsoil is a top B-deficiency trigger.
Avoid over-liming (locks B).
Build organic matter (B reservoir).
Foliar B at flowering for set-critical crops.
Don't over-apply K/Ca which antagonise B.
On sandy/leached soils, split or foliar B (it leaches fast).
Test soil/water B before applying (toxicity risk).
Use boronated blends for even low-dose distribution.
commonFarmerMistakes: Exceeding the borax dose — easy toxicity (narrow margin).
Uneven borax spread creating toxic hot spots that kill plants in patches.
Confusing B deficiency (young/growing point/fruit) with B toxicity (old-leaf margin scorch).
Treating cauliflower browning/hollow-stem as disease instead of B.
Over-liming and locking up B.
Ignoring dry-topsoil as a B-uptake blocker.
Not applying B in crucifers/oilseeds.
Heavy K/Ca antagonising B.
Re-applying B without a soil test.
Using high-B irrigation water (adds to toxicity).
Spot-applying borax (concentration burns).
Not dissolving borax properly (uneven).
Applying B to dry soil (no uptake).
Assuming more B improves set (over-dose backfires).
Ignoring flowering-stage B in fruit/seed crops.
expertTips: Respect boron's NARROW margin — measure doses precisely, never eyeball.
Mix borax with sand/FYM for even spread; hot spots kill plants.
For cauliflower browning/hollow stem: 0.1-0.2% borax foliar at curd initiation + basal borax.
Keep topsoil moist — B uptake fails in dry topsoil even when soil B is adequate.
Foliar B at flowering is the safest, most targeted correction.
Diagnose: B DEFICIENCY on young/growing point/fruit; B TOXICITY on OLD-leaf margins.
Avoid over-liming — it locks B.
Don't stack heavy K/Ca with low B (antagonism).
On sandy soils, B leaches fast — split or foliar.
Use boronated NPK/SSP blends for safe even low-dose B.
Dissolve borax in warm water for foliar.
Test soil/water B before soil application (toxicity is worse than deficiency).
Groundnut hollow-heart and fruit cracking are hidden B signs.
Build organic matter as a B buffer.
New growth/flowers respond — judge success there.
Solubor/boric acid dissolve better than borax for foliar.
Time B to pre-flowering/curding for set-critical crops.
On high-B irrigation water, avoid extra B and leach if scorch appears.
Never re-apply B 'to be safe' — confirm need.
Log B applications per plot to avoid cumulative toxicity.

faq.q: Cauliflower ki curd brown + tana hollow kyun?
faq.a: Boron ki kami. Basal borax 4-5 kg/acre + curd initiation par 0.1-0.2% borax foliar; nami sthir rakhein.
Basal 4-5 kg/acre (crucifer/oilseed); foliar 0.1-0.2%. Dose se zyada bilkul nahi — toxic ho jata hai.

faq.q: B deficiency aur toxicity me farak?
faq.a: Deficiency: nayi patti/growing point/phal kharab; Toxicity: PURANI patti ke kinare jalna (brown scorch).

faq.q: Borax patchy kyun jal deta hai?
faq.a: Uneven spread se hot spots. Sand/FYM ke saath achhi tarah mila kar dein.
Haan — B uptake ke liye topsoil nami zaroori; sukhi topsoil me B nahi milta.
pH badhne se B lock ho jata hai.
Flowering par 0.1-0.2% borax/boric acid foliar set sudharta hai.
Foliar 7-14 din.
Foliar ke liye boric acid/Solubor behtar ghulnsheel; basal me borax.
B ki kami — kernel andar dark/hollow. B dein.
Haan — toxicity deficiency se bhi buri; soil/water test karein.
Jaldi leach hota hai — split ya foliar dein.
Nahi — B deficiency; fungicide bekaar.
Even low-dose B ke liye surakshit — hot spots nahi.
Antagonism se B uptake girta hai.
Garm paani me ghol kar foliar banayein.
B band karein, light soil me irrigation se leach karein, organic matter dein.
Haan, B + steady nami se cracking/corking kam hoti hai.

nutrients.name: Calcium
nutrients.mobility: IMMOBILE in plant (deficiency on YOUNG leaves & growing points, fruit tips first)

overview.whyImportant: Calcium is the cement of cell walls and the regulator of new-tissue growth. It moves only with the transpiration stream, so deficiency hits the newest growth and fruit even when soil Ca is adequate. Responsible for blossom-end rot, tip-burn, and fruit cracking — disorders farmers routinely mistake for disease.

plantFunctions.roleInsidePlant: Cell-wall (calcium pectate) structure, membrane stability, cell division at growing points.
plantFunctions.photosynthesis: Indirect via membrane and enzyme integrity.
plantFunctions.rootDevelopment: Essential for root-tip growth; deficiency kills root tips.
plantFunctions.flowering: Supports pollen tube growth and fruit set.
plantFunctions.fruitDevelopment: Critical — prevents blossom-end rot, cracking, tip-burn; firms fruit and extends shelf-life.
plantFunctions.enzymeActivation: Second-messenger in signalling; activates some enzymes.
plantFunctions.yieldContribution: Mainly QUALITY — firm, crack-free, storable fruit and healthy growing points.

whyDeficiencyHappens.why: Ca usually adequate but uptake blocked by other issues; sodic soils have Na displacing Ca.
Acid soils are genuinely low in Ca; Al/Mn toxicity harms roots.
Low Ca reserves; leaching.
Leaches Ca from light soils, lowers pH.
Poor transpiration cuts Ca movement to new tissue.
Fluctuating water disrupts the transpiration stream that carries Ca to fruit — main cause of blossom-end rot.
Poorer soil structure and Ca cycling.

whyDeficiencyHappens.condition: Nutrient imbalance / excess K, Mg, NH4, Na
whyDeficiencyHappens.why: These cations antagonise and block Ca uptake.
High K competes strongly with Ca uptake — common in over-fertigated fruit crops.
Ca is taken up only by young root tips; damaged roots stop Ca uptake.
Slows transpiration and root activity.
Low transpiration (dry root zone or humid air) stops Ca reaching fruit/new leaves.
Na competes with and displaces Ca.
Heavy K/N with no Ca, and irregular irrigation, trigger localised Ca deficiency in fruit.

symptoms.note: Ca is IMMOBILE — deficiency shows in YOUNG leaves, growing tips and FRUIT, never old leaves.
symptoms.early: New leaves distorted, cupped, hooked tips; growing point weak.
symptoms.middle: Death of growing point, deformed young leaves, blossom-end rot appearing on fruit.
symptoms.severe: Growing point dies (blackens), young leaves scorched/hooked, fruit blossom-end rot & cracking, root-tip death.
symptoms.oldLeaves: Stay normal — Ca is not relocated from them.
symptoms.youngLeaves: First affected: distorted, cupped, chlorotic/scorched margins, hooked tips.
symptoms.stem: Weak growing tips; internal tip-burn in cabbage.
symptoms.root: Root-tip death, stubby brown roots, poor new-root growth.
symptoms.flower: Poor pollen-tube growth and fruit set.
symptoms.fruit: Blossom-end rot (sunken black leathery base), cracking, bitter pit-type disorders, poor firmness/shelf-life.
symptoms.seed: Poor seed development in severe cases.
symptoms.wholePlant: Healthy old leaves but dying tips, deformed new growth and disordered fruit — the top-down pattern.

cropWiseSymptoms.symptoms: Rare; on acid soils, white/rolled young leaves, poor root tips.
On acid soils, terminal-bud death, poor nodulation, deformed young leaves.
POPS (empty pods) and poor kernel fill from low Ca in the pegging zone — the classic groundnut Ca problem; fix with gypsum.
Blossom-end rot on fruit, hooked/distorted young leaves, tip die-back under moisture swings.
Blossom-end rot (sunken dark fruit base) — very common in polyhouse with irregular irrigation/high K.
Tip-burn of inner young leaves, deformed curd margins.
Internal TIP-BURN — brown scorched inner leaf margins inside the head (looks fine outside).
Blossom-end rot, tip-burn of young leaves, mis-shapen fruit under moisture stress.
Blossom-end rot, distorted young leaves, fruit cracking.
Distorted young leaves, poor tip growth, pod-tip disorders.
Young leaves stuck together (ladder/tip-hooking), gelatinous tips, poor emergence of new leaves.
Rare; on acid/sandy soils poor young-leaf and root-tip growth.
On acid soils, terminal-bud damage, poor nodulation.
On acid soils, growing-point death, poor root tips.
On acid soils, young-leaf tip die-back, weak spindle, poor root system.

howToFix.soilApplicationDose: 100 kg/acre (groundnut at pegging); 200-400 kg/acre for sodic-soil reclamation
howToFix.bestCropStage: Groundnut flowering-pegging; basal for others; sodic reclamation pre-sowing
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Broadcast + incorporate; groundnut: apply in pegging zone
howToFix.precautions: Neutral salt — safe; also supplies sulphur; ideal for sodic soils and groundnut.

howToFix.foliarSprayDose: 4-5 g/L (0.4-0.5%) — the standard blossom-end-rot / tip-burn fix
howToFix.bestCropStage: Early fruit development; repeat weekly on BER-prone crops
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Foliar (direct to fruit/new growth) or fertigation
howToFix.expectedRecoveryTime: New fruit protected; existing BER fruit won't recover
howToFix.precautions: Never tank-mix with phosphate or sulphate/KNO3 (precipitates). Spray directly on fruit zone — Ca won't move there from leaves.

howToFix.soilApplicationDose: Per lime requirement, 200-800 kg/acre on acid soils
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Broadcast + incorporate 2-4 weeks before sowing
howToFix.precautions: Corrects acidity AND supplies Ca; dolomite adds Mg too.

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Foliar/fertigation for fast uptake
howToFix.precautions: Higher cost; for premium crops.

methodOfApplication.broadcast: Lime/gypsum broadcast + incorporate for soil Ca and acidity/sodicity correction.
methodOfApplication.foliarSpray: Calcium nitrate 0.5% sprayed DIRECTLY on fruit/new growth — the only fast fix for BER/tip-burn (Ca won't relocate).
methodOfApplication.dripFertigation: CaNO3 through drip (keep separate from phosphate/sulphate lines).

toxicity.whatHappens: Excess Ca (over-liming) raises pH and induces micronutrient (Fe, Mn, Zn, B) and K/Mg deficiencies.
toxicity.plantToxicitySymptoms: Interveinal chlorosis of young leaves (induced Fe/Mn), poor micronutrient uptake.
toxicity.correctionMethods: Stop liming; apply chelated Fe/Mn/Zn; add sulphur/acidifier to lower pH; balance cations.

prevention: Lime acid soils to correct low Ca at the source.
Apply gypsum in groundnut at pegging and in sodic soils.
Keep irrigation STEADY (drip + mulch) — erratic moisture is the main BER trigger.
Don't over-apply K/N which antagonise Ca in fruit.
Foliar CaNO3 preventively on BER-prone crops (tomato/chilli/capsicum) from early fruit set.
Maintain good root health (Ca uptake needs live root tips).
Improve drainage; avoid waterlogging.
Use dolomite where both Ca and Mg are low.
Manage humidity/ventilation in polyhouse to keep transpiration adequate.
Soil-test pH and base saturation periodically.
commonFarmerMistakes: Treating blossom-end rot as a fungal disease and spraying fungicide.
Spraying calcium on leaves expecting it to reach fruit (it won't — spray the fruit zone).
Erratic irrigation (dry-then-flood) causing BER despite adequate soil Ca.
Over-applying K/N in fertigation, blocking Ca uptake.
Not liming acid soils.
Tank-mixing CaNO3 with phosphate/sulphate/KNO3 (precipitates, clogs).
Ignoring groundnut pegging-zone gypsum, getting pops/empty pods.
Over-liming and inducing Fe/Mn/Zn deficiency.
Assuming high soil Ca means no deficiency (it's a transport problem).
Poor drainage killing root tips that take up Ca.
High humidity in polyhouse cutting transpiration and Ca flow.
Diagnosing cabbage tip-burn only at harvest (cut heads to check early).
Applying Ca too late (after BER appears) — damaged fruit won't recover.
Not using mulch to stabilise soil moisture.
Skipping soil test and cation balance.
expertTips: For BER: spray 0.5% Calcium nitrate DIRECTLY on the fruit zone from early fruit set, weekly.
Fix the ROOT CAUSE of BER — steady moisture — not just spray Ca.
In groundnut, apply 100 kg gypsum/acre at flowering-pegging for pod fill.
Never tank-mix Ca with phosphate or sulphate fertilisers.
Use dolomite when soil is low in both Ca and Mg.
Lime acid soils 2-4 weeks before sowing for Ca + pH.
Keep K and N moderate in fruit crops to protect Ca uptake.
Ca moves only to high-transpiration parts — old leaves get it, fruit often doesn't; spray fruit.
In polyhouse, ventilate to maintain transpiration and Ca delivery.
Mulch to prevent the moisture swings that cause BER/cracking.
Check cabbage heads early for internal tip-burn, don't wait for harvest.
Maintain live white root tips — they are the only Ca-uptake sites.
Use gypsum (not lime) when you need Ca without raising pH.
On sodic soils, gypsum supplies Ca and displaces Na.
Balance the Ca:Mg:K ratio via soil test.
Chelated Ca foliar for premium veg needing fast uptake.
Avoid waterlogging — it stops Ca movement.
Preventive Ca beats curative — start before symptoms.
Watch young leaves and growing tips for the earliest Ca signs.
Keep a moisture + Ca-spray log on BER-prone crops.

faq.q: Tamatar/capsicum ke phal ke neeche kaala sadan (BER) kyun?
faq.a: Calcium ki kami + asमान sinchai. 0.5% Calcium nitrate seedhe phal par chhidkein aur drip+mulch se nami sthir rakhein.

faq.q: Calcium patti par spray karun ya phal par?
faq.a: Phal/nayi growth par — Ca patti se phal tak nahi jata (immobile).
Pegging zone me calcium ki kami. Flowering par 100 kg gypsum/acre dein.

faq.q: CaNO3 ko kisi aur khaad ke saath mila sakte hain?
faq.a: Phosphate/sulphate/KNO3 ke saath nahi — precipitate (jam) ho jata hai.

faq.q: Purani patti theek, nayi kharab — kya?
faq.a: Ca immobile hai, isliye kami hamesha nayi patti/growing tip/phal me dikhti hai.
Lime pH badhata hai + Ca deta hai (acid soil ke liye); gypsum pH badhaye bina Ca+sulphur deta hai (groundnut/sodic soil).

faq.q: Cabbage ka andar ka kinara brown kyun?
faq.a: Internal tip-burn — calcium ki kami + tezi se growth/nami swing. Steady nami + CaNO3.
Nahi — khraab phal recover nahi hote; naye phal ko bachane ke liye pehle se Ca spray karein.

faq.q: Zyada K se Ca ruk jata hai?
faq.a: Haan — zyada K/N Ca uptake block karte hain. Fruit crop me balance rakhein.
High humidity + irregular fertigation transpiration kam karta hai, Ca phal tak nahi pahunchta.

faq.q: Ca deficiency ki jad kya hai?
faq.a: Aksar mitti me Ca hota hai par nami/uptake problem se phal tak nahi pahunchta — nami sthir karein.
Lime se — pH bhi sudhrega aur Ca bhi milega.
Gypsum se — Ca deta hai aur sodium hatata hai.
Nami sthir rakhta hai, jisse BER/cracking kam hoti hai.
Fruit set ke shuru se, symptom aane ka intezar mat karein.
pH bahut badh kar Fe/Mn/Zn ki kami ho jati hai (nayi patti pili).
High-value veg me fast uptake ke liye.
Steady nami + adequate Ca — dono zaroori.

faq.q: Root health se Ca ka kya rishta?
faq.a: Ca sirf naye safed root tips se milta hai; kharab jad = Ca uptake band.
Jab Ca aur Mg dono kam hon.

nutrients.name: Chlorine
nutrients.mobility: Highly mobile (deficiency rare; toxicity/salinity far more common in India)

overview.whyImportant: Chlorine (as chloride) is essential in small amounts for the water-splitting step of photosynthesis, osmotic/stomatal regulation and disease suppression. In India, chloride DEFICIENCY is very rare — chloride TOXICITY/salinity is the real, common field problem.

plantFunctions.roleInsidePlant: Osmotic regulation, stomatal function, charge balance, part of the photosynthetic oxygen-evolving complex.
plantFunctions.photosynthesis: Required for the water-splitting (O2-evolving) reaction (with Mn).
plantFunctions.yieldContribution: Small direct need; some disease-suppression benefit; excess reduces yield via salinity.

whyDeficiencyHappens.why: Chloride is highly leachable; only heavy-rainfall, low-Cl inland areas risk deficiency (rare).

whyDeficiencyHappens.condition: Sandy leached soils far from sea
whyDeficiencyHappens.why: Low chloride input; but atmospheric/rain Cl usually suffices.
Deficiency essentially only where irrigation water, rain and fertilisers all lack Cl — uncommon in India.
Most Indian issues are chloride TOXICITY from saline soils/water and heavy MOP use, NOT deficiency.

symptoms.note: Deficiency VERY RARE. When it occurs: wilting, chlorosis, and stubby roots. Far more relevant is chloride TOXICITY (leaf-margin scorch).
symptoms.early: (Deficiency) mild wilting of young leaves even with moisture.
symptoms.root: Stubby, restricted, club-like roots (classic Cl-deficiency sign).
symptoms.wholePlant: Wilty, chlorotic, stubby-rooted (deficiency); OR — much more common — leaf-margin scorch and stunting from chloride TOXICITY/salinity.

cropWiseSymptoms.symptoms: Deficiency rare; salinity/chloride toxicity causes tip scorch and stunting far more often.
Chloride-sensitive — TOXICITY (leaf-margin scorch) is the concern, not deficiency.
Sensitive to chloride/salinity; margin scorch under excess.
Chloride/salt toxicity leaf-margin scorch under saline water; deficiency not seen.
Salt/chloride sensitivity in fertigation; margin scorch.
Deficiency not seen; salt tolerance moderate.
Relatively chloride-tolerant; deficiency not seen.
Salt-sensitive; chloride excess causes margin scorch.
Moderate tolerance; excess causes scorch.
Moderate tolerance; excess causes margin scorch.
Deficiency rare; salinity reduces growth.
Salt-tolerant crop; deficiency not seen.
Chloride-sensitive; excess scorch.
Sensitive to salinity/chloride excess.
Moderate tolerance; excess chloride reduces growth/quality.

howToFix.soilApplicationDose: Normal K rate provides ample chloride
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Routine MOP use supplies more than enough Cl — deliberate Cl fertilisation is almost never needed in India
howToFix.precautions: Do not add chloride specifically; MOP already provides it. On saline/chloride-sensitive crops, switch to SOP.

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Ambient chloride from rain/irrigation usually meets crop need
howToFix.precautions: Test water — many Indian waters are HIGH in chloride (toxicity risk).

methodOfApplication.broadcast: MOP (routine K) provides chloride; no separate Cl application needed.

toxicity.whatHappens: The REAL issue in India — chloride toxicity from saline soils/water and heavy MOP.
toxicity.plantToxicitySymptoms: Leaf-margin/tip SCORCH (burning), bronzing, leaf drop, stunting; worst in chloride-sensitive crops (soybean, groundnut, chilli, cucurbits).
toxicity.yieldLoss: Significant on saline soils and with poor-quality irrigation water.
toxicity.qualityReduction: Scorch reduces photosynthesis and marketable quality.
toxicity.correctionMethods: Use SOP instead of MOP on sensitive/saline situations; leach salts with good water; improve drainage; gypsum for sodic soils; choose tolerant crops/varieties; test irrigation water.

prevention: Don't deliberately apply chloride — routine MOP and water supply enough.
On saline soils / chloride-sensitive crops, use SOP not MOP.
Test irrigation water for chloride/salinity.
Leach salts with good-quality water where possible.
Improve drainage on salt-affected soils.
Use gypsum on sodic soils.
Choose salt-tolerant crops/varieties on saline land.
Avoid over-MOP on sensitive crops.
Build organic matter to buffer salinity.
Monitor for margin-scorch (toxicity), not deficiency.
commonFarmerMistakes: Worrying about chloride deficiency (essentially non-existent in India).
Using MOP on chloride-sensitive/saline crops instead of SOP.
Ignoring irrigation-water chloride/salinity testing.
Confusing chloride-toxicity margin scorch with potassium-deficiency scorch or disease.
Over-applying MOP and adding to salt load.
Not leaching salts on saline soils.
Poor drainage worsening salinity.
Growing sensitive crops on saline land.
Not using gypsum on sodic soils.
Assuming all leaf-edge scorch is potassium-related.
Using high-chloride water without management.
Ignoring crop tolerance differences.
Adding chloride 'nutrient' products needlessly.
Not building organic matter on salty soils.
Overlooking salinity as the real yield limiter.
expertTips: In India, manage chloride TOXICITY/salinity — deficiency is a non-issue.
Use SOP instead of MOP on saline soils and chloride-sensitive crops (soybean, groundnut, chilli, cucurbits).
Test irrigation water for chloride/EC before the season.
Leach accumulated salts with good-quality water when available.
Improve drainage on salt-affected fields.
Use gypsum to reclaim sodic soils.
Pick salt-tolerant crops/varieties for saline land.
Diagnose chloride toxicity by leaf-MARGIN scorch + bronzing.
Don't over-apply MOP on sensitive crops.
Build organic matter to buffer salinity.
Chloride from routine MOP + rain meets crop need — never fertilise Cl deliberately.
Distinguish Cl-toxicity scorch from K-deficiency scorch (context + water test).
Mulch to reduce surface salt accumulation.

Chasing Ni instead of proven agronomy.
expertTips: In Indian field crops, do NOT fertilise nickel — focus on the real limiting nutrients.
Ni's job is running UREASE (urea breakdown) — normal soils supply plenty.
The practical Ni concern is TOXICITY/contamination, not deficiency.
Avoid Ni-contaminated sewage sludge/industrial waste near food crops.
Lime acid soils to keep Ni locked and safe.
Build organic matter to buffer heavy metals.
Balance Fe/Zn to counter Ni-induced chlorosis on contaminated soils.
Test soils near industry for heavy metals.
Diagnose leaf-tip necrosis carefully — rarely Ni in field crops.
Use phytoremediation on genuinely Ni-contaminated land.
Prioritise N/P/K/S/Zn/Fe/B — these actually limit Indian yields.
Keep produce within heavy-metal food-safety limits.
Only research/perennial-nut crops abroad show true Ni deficiency.
Maintain good soil pH for overall micronutrient safety.
Never add exotic Ni products 'to be safe'.
Manage urea well (splits, incorporation) — no Ni needed for that.
Consult a soil scientist before any Ni use.
Watch for Ni in mining/industrial catchment irrigation water.
Organic amendments reduce heavy-metal bioavailability.
Treat Ni as a contamination-management topic, not a fertiliser.

faq.a: Nahi — Indian field crops me mitti paryapt Ni deti hai. Ni ki deficiency lagbhag nahi hoti; asli chinta toxicity/contamination hai.
Urease enzyme ka cofactor — urea ko usable N me todta hai; N-fixation ke hydrogenase me bhi.
Field crops me lagbhag nahi — doosri wajah dhundein; diagnosis ke bina Ni mat daalein.
Contaminated sludge/industrial input se; iron/zinc deficiency induce, stunting, food-safety khatra.
Contaminated sludge avoid, acid soil me lime, organic matter, Fe/Zn balance.
Mostly perennial/nut crops (videsh) me research me; Indian field crops me nahi.
Industrial area/contaminated soil me heavy-metal test karein.

faq.q: Ni ki jagah kis par dhyan?
faq.a: N, P, K, S, Zn, Fe, B — ye actually yield limit karte hain.
Mitti me paryapt Ni hota hai; alag se dena zaroori nahi.
Haan — excess toxic, food-safety issue.
Mining/industrial catchment paani me Ni ho sakta hai — avoid.
Heavy-metal bioavailability kam karta hai.
pH badhne se Ni lock/safe ho jata hai.
Contaminated land par metal-absorbing plants se safai.
Bilkul nahi — toxicity risk.
Fe/Zn dein, lime, organic matter.
Produce me heavy-metal limit ke andar rakhein.
Ni use se pehle zaroor salah lein.
Haan, sabse kam maatra me — par soils already supply karti hain.

references: ICAR; IARI; peer-reviewed plant-nutrition literature on nickel/urease
ICAR-CSSRI (heavy-metal/contamination); SAU Package of Practices; FCO specs

nutrients.source: ICAR / IARI / SAU Package of Practices

overview.whyImportant: Nitrogen is the engine of plant growth and the nutrient most often limiting yield in Indian soils, which are largely low in organic matter. It is the core building block of chlorophyll (green colour + photosynthesis), all proteins, enzymes, nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) and is directly responsible for vegetative vigour, tillering/branching, leaf area, and grain protein. A shortfall shows up faster and hits yield harder than almost any other nutrient, which is why India consumes more urea than any other fertiliser.

plantFunctions.roleInsidePlant: Structural and functional core: N is part of every amino acid, hence every protein and enzyme; part of chlorophyll, ATP, ADP, and nucleic acids.
plantFunctions.photosynthesis: N builds chlorophyll and the Rubisco enzyme that fixes CO2. Adequate N = dark green leaves, large leaf area, high photosynthetic rate. Deficient plants are pale and photosynthesise poorly.
plantFunctions.rootDevelopment: Moderate N promotes healthy root growth, but EXCESS N pushes shoot growth at the expense of roots (high shoot:root ratio), making plants lodging- and drought-prone.
plantFunctions.vegetativeGrowth: Drives tillering in cereals (paddy, wheat, bajra), branching in pulses/oilseeds, and leaf/canopy expansion. This is N's most visible role.
plantFunctions.flowering: Adequate N supports flower initiation and number; excess N delays flowering and causes vegetative over-growth with poor flower set.
plantFunctions.fruitDevelopment: Supports fruit size and number via sustained canopy and photosynthate supply; excess N delays maturity and reduces fruit quality/colour/shelf-life.
plantFunctions.proteinSynthesis: N is essential for amino acid and protein formation; directly raises grain/seed protein content (e.g. wheat, pulses) and fodder quality.
plantFunctions.enzymeActivation: As a component of enzymes themselves, N underlies virtually all metabolic reactions.
plantFunctions.chlorophyllFormation: N is a central atom-group in the chlorophyll molecule; the classic deficiency sign (yellowing) is loss of chlorophyll.
plantFunctions.yieldContribution: Highest single-nutrient yield response in most Indian crops. Determines number of tillers/ears, grains per ear, leaf area duration and thus total dry-matter and economic yield.

whyDeficiencyHappens.why: Ammonia volatilisation is high in alkaline soils; surface-applied urea/ammonium loses N as ammonia gas before the plant can use it.
Reduced microbial nitrification and poor organic-matter mineralisation slow the release of plant-available N; acidic soils often have low N-supplying capacity.
Low cation-exchange and water-holding capacity let nitrate leach quickly below the root zone with irrigation or rain.
Nitrate is highly water-soluble and leaches down; in wet soils it is also lost as gas through denitrification.
Anaerobic (oxygen-less) conditions trigger denitrification, converting nitrate to N2/N2O gas that escapes to the air; roots also stop absorbing.
Over-irrigation flushes mobile nitrate out of the root zone, mimicking heavy-rain leaching losses.
Most Indian soils are low in organic carbon; since a large share of soil N comes from mineralising organic matter, low OM = low N-supplying power.
Very high N without balanced P, K, S limits uptake efficiency; conversely deficiencies of S/Mo impair N metabolism so plants show N-hunger even when N is present.
Heavy DAP-only regimes skew NP ratio; luxuriant early P can mask and later exaggerate relative N shortfall as the crop bulks up.
Very high K can antagonise N uptake balance and, with poor scheduling, contribute to apparent N stress.
Compacted/shallow/diseased roots cannot forage the soil volume needed to capture mobile nitrate, causing deficiency even in adequately fertilised fields.
Low temperature slows microbial mineralisation and nitrification, reducing available N in early Rabi/winter; young crops look pale until soil warms.
N uptake needs soil moisture (mass flow); in dry soil, applied urea does not dissolve/move to roots and mineralisation stalls.
High salts reduce water and nutrient uptake and impair root function, lowering effective N absorption.
Full-dose basal urea (instead of splits), broadcasting on dry/hot surface without incorporation, and wrong timing all cause huge volatilisation/leaching losses so little reaches the plant.

symptoms.note: Nitrogen is MOBILE in the plant: the plant moves N from old leaves to new growth, so deficiency ALWAYS starts on the OLDEST (lower) leaves.
symptoms.early: Overall lighter/pale-green colour, slightly reduced vigour and slower growth; crop looks 'hungry' and uneven.
symptoms.middle: Distinct yellowing (chlorosis) of lower/older leaves starting from the leaf TIP and moving down the midrib in a V-shape (classic in maize/paddy); fewer tillers/branches, thin stand.
symptoms.severe: Older leaves turn fully yellow then brown/dry and die from the tip; stunted plant, spindly stems, drastically reduced tillering, early maturity, very low yield.
symptoms.oldLeaves: First and worst affected: uniform pale-yellow, tip-and-midrib V-yellowing, then scorching/death. This bottom-up pattern is the key diagnostic.
symptoms.youngLeaves: Stay greener longer because the plant relocates N upward; young leaves yellow only in very severe, prolonged deficiency.
symptoms.stem: Thin, weak, spindly; may show reddish/purplish tinge in some crops (e.g. maize) as sugars accumulate; short internodes, reduced branching.
symptoms.root: Under mild deficiency roots may elongate searching for N; under severe deficiency overall root mass and vigour drop with the whole plant.
symptoms.flower: Fewer flowers, delayed or reduced flowering, poor flower retention due to weak canopy and low photosynthate.
symptoms.fruit: Small, few fruits; pale colour; in cereals fewer grains per ear; low fruit/grain number is the main yield hit.
symptoms.seed: Shrivelled, low-weight grain; reduced grain/seed protein content and poor quality.
symptoms.wholePlant: Pale, stunted, thin uneven stand that matures early; lower leaves dying upward while top stays paler-green; sharply reduced biomass and yield.

cropWiseSymptoms.symptoms: Pale yellow-green crop, poor/late tillering, thin stand; older leaves yellow from tip along midrib then dry; short plants, small panicles, fewer filled grains. Whole-field paleness after transplanting shock or on sandy/leached bunds.
Pale green overall, older-leaf yellowing; because it is a legume, true N-hunger often signals POOR NODULATION (no Rhizobium) rather than soil N shortage — check root nodules before piling on urea.
General light-green colour, older-leaf yellowing, weak growth; again a legume — poor nodulation is a common cause; confirm nodules before N application.
Older leaves pale then yellow, plant stunted with thin stems, fewer branches, poor flowering and small fruit; reduced picking.
Light-green older leaves, slow canopy build-up, weak plants, poor fruit size; in fertigated polyhouse crops usually from skipped/low N in the nutrient solution.
Pale, slow-growing plants with small leaves; leads to SMALL curds and often BUTTONING (tiny premature curd); older leaves yellow first.
Pale small outer leaves, slow head formation, loose small heads, uneven stand; older leaves yellow and drop.
Pale older leaves, slow weak vines, poor fruit set, small pale fruit; yellowing progresses up the vine from the base.
Older leaves pale-yellow, stunted bushy-but-weak plants, fewer flowers and small fruit; slow recovery after each picking.
Pale lower leaves yellowing upward, thin short plants, fewer nodes/flowers, small fibrous pods, low picking.
Textbook V-shaped yellowing from the TIP of lower leaves along the midrib; stalks thin, sometimes reddish/purple; poor cob fill, tip barrenness. The clearest N-deficiency picture of all crops.
Pale thin crop, poor tillering, older-leaf yellowing; on sandy dryland soils appears quickly; small earheads and low grain set.
Light-green plants, older-leaf yellowing; legume — usually poor nodulation/biofertiliser miss; confirm nodules; excess N actually reduces nodulation.
Pale slow early growth, older-leaf yellowing; legume with slow nodulation — a small starter N helps but heavy N suppresses nodules; check roots.
Pale yellow-green foliage, thin canes, poor tillering, older leaves yellow and dry from tip; stunted grand-growth, low tonnage and sugar. Very N-responsive crop.

howToFix.soilApplicationDose: Per crop schedule, e.g. cereals ~50-120 kg/acre total N split; as a corrective top-dress 25-45 kg urea/acre
howToFix.foliarSprayDose: 2% solution = 20 g per litre (roughly 400 g in 20 L pump); ~3-4 kg urea in 150-200 L/acre
howToFix.fertigationDose: 3-5 kg/acre per fertigation split, dissolved and injected
howToFix.waterQuantity: Foliar: 150-200 L/acre (veg), 200 L/acre; dissolve fully before spray
howToFix.bestCropStage: Active vegetative growth / tillering / pre-flowering; top-dress at active growth stages
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Soil top-dress into moist soil then light irrigation (to wash in and cut volatilisation); or 2% foliar for fast rescue; or fertigation
howToFix.numberOfApplications: Soil: 2-3 splits over season; foliar rescue: 2-3 sprays
howToFix.expectedRecoveryTime: Foliar greening in 4-7 days; soil top-dress response in 7-15 days with moisture
howToFix.precautions: Never broadcast on dry/hot soil surface (heavy ammonia loss) — incorporate or irrigate. Do not exceed 2% foliar (leaf scorch). Do not mix urea foliar with strongly alkaline products.

howToFix.soilApplicationDose: Same as urea; slower release improves use-efficiency
howToFix.foliarSprayDose: Not used for foliar; soil only
howToFix.fertigationDose: Not preferred for fertigation (coating); use plain urea/water-soluble for fertigation
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Soil band/top-dress + incorporate/irrigate
howToFix.precautions: This is the standard subsidised urea in India; still incorporate to reduce losses.

howToFix.soilApplicationDose: 40-80 kg/acre as top-dress depending on crop
howToFix.bestCropStage: Top-dressing; good on acidic soils and for quick nitrate uptake
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Soil top-dress; less volatilisation than urea; good in cold/wet early season
howToFix.precautions: Store dry (hygroscopic); avoid on already alkaline-sodic if calcium excess is a concern.

howToFix.soilApplicationDose: 50-100 kg/acre (also supplies sulphur — good for oilseeds/pulses)
howToFix.bestCropStage: Basal/early top-dress, especially S-needing crops and alkaline soils
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Soil; acidifying effect helps in alkaline/calcareous soils
howToFix.precautions: Acidifying — avoid heavy repeated use on already acidic soils.

howToFix.soilApplicationDose: 40-50 kg/acre basal (mainly a P source; supplies starter N)
howToFix.foliarSprayDose: 2% (20 g/L) foliar for combined N+P boost at flowering
howToFix.bestCropStage: Basal at sowing/transplant; foliar at flower initiation
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Basal band placement near seed/root zone; or foliar
howToFix.precautions: Do not rely on DAP alone for N; balance with urea top-dress. Band-place, do not surface-broadcast in dry soil.

howToFix.bestCropStage: Early vegetative to flowering in vegetables/fertigated crops
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Drip fertigation or foliar; ideal for polyhouse capsicum/cucumber
howToFix.precautions: Use clean-dissolving grades to avoid drip clogging; check EC.

howToFix.foliarSprayDose: 1% (10 g/L) foliar; nitrate N + K, good under stress
howToFix.fertigationDose: 2-4 kg/acre per split, especially fruiting stage
howToFix.bestCropStage: Flowering to fruiting (supplies nitrate-N + K together)
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Fertigation or foliar
howToFix.precautions: Do not tank-mix with calcium products (precipitate).

howToFix.foliarSprayDose: NA (jeevamrut/panchagavya used as soil/foliar biostimulant, not primary N)
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Broadcast + incorporate; builds long-term N-supplying capacity
howToFix.expectedRecoveryTime: Slow, season-long release (not for acute rescue)
howToFix.precautions: Use well-decomposed material; raw manure ties up N and burns roots.

howToFix.soilApplicationDose: Seed treatment or soil 2-4 kg/acre with FYM
howToFix.bestCropStage: Seed treatment at sowing; Rhizobium is CRITICAL for soybean/moong/arhar/groundnut
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Seed treatment (legumes) / soil application with FYM (cereals with Azotobacter/Azospirillum)
howToFix.precautions: Do not mix biofertiliser with chemical fungicide/fertiliser at same time on seed; keep out of direct sun; use before expiry.

methodOfApplication.broadcast: Even spreading over the field, then incorporate/irrigate. Use for basal N and large-area top-dressing in cereals. Never leave urea on dry hot surface — wash in to avoid ammonia loss.
methodOfApplication.bandPlacement: Place fertiliser in bands ~5 cm beside and below the seed/row. Best for basal DAP/complex and for reducing N contact-loss; improves early uptake.
methodOfApplication.spotApplication: Place a measured dose near the base of individual plants (widely-spaced veg, sugarcane, orchards). Efficient, low-waste for high-value crops.
methodOfApplication.foliarSpray: 2% urea (20 g/L) sprayed on foliage for FAST rescue of visible deficiency, at flowering, or when soil is dry/cold and roots can't take up N. Fastest greening but supplements, not replaces, soil N.
methodOfApplication.dripFertigation: Dissolve water-soluble N (urea, 19:19:19, KNO3) and deliver through drip in small frequent doses. Highest use-efficiency; standard for vegetables, polyhouse capsicum/cucumber, sugarcane drip.
methodOfApplication.rootDrench: Pour dilute N solution at the root zone of transplants/nursery for quick establishment and uniform early growth.
methodOfApplication.seedTreatment: Not for chemical N, but Rhizobium/Azotobacter/Azospirillum biofertiliser seed coating supplies biological N — essential for legumes (soybean, moong, arhar, groundnut).

toxicity.whatHappens: Excess N causes rank, dark-green, over-vegetative growth with soft weak tissue at the cost of flowering, fruiting, and quality.
toxicity.plantToxicitySymptoms: Very dark blue-green leaves, excessive leafiness, soft succulent stems, delayed flowering/maturity, LODGING (falling over) in cereals/sugarcane, and higher pest/disease susceptibility (aphids, borers, blights love lush N-rich tissue). In leafy/veg crops, nitrate accumulation raises food-safety concern.
toxicity.yieldLoss: Grain crops lodge and shed; fruit crops set poorly and mature late; the classic 'all leaves no yield' outcome. Net yield can fall despite (because of) high N.
toxicity.qualityReduction: Poor fruit colour and lower sugar/TSS, reduced shelf-life, delayed/uneven ripening, hollow or watery produce, higher nitrate in leafy veg, lower malting/milling quality.
toxicity.correctionMethods: Stop further N immediately; apply extra K (and P) to rebalance and firm tissue; give a light-load stress (controlled irrigation) to slow growth; support/prop lodging-prone crops; increase pest/disease scouting; on next crop, cut N dose and switch to splits.

prevention: Soil-test before the season and apply N as per the recommendation, not by habit.
Apply N in SPLITS (basal + 2-3 top-dressings) instead of one big dose — the single biggest efficiency gain.
Use neem-coated / slow-release urea and always incorporate or irrigate after top-dressing.
Build soil organic matter with FYM/compost/green manure (dhaincha) to raise natural N-supplying capacity.
Treat all legume seed with Rhizobium and cereals with Azotobacter/Azospirillum.
Match N timing to crop demand (tillering, active growth, pre-flowering) and to soil moisture.
Use Leaf Colour Chart (LCC) or SPAD in paddy/wheat to apply N only when the crop signals need.
Avoid over-irrigation and improve drainage to cut leaching and denitrification losses.
Balance N with adequate P, K and especially SULPHUR (oilseeds/pulses) and Zn.
On sandy soils, apply smaller, more frequent doses; on alkaline soils, incorporate urea to stop volatilisation.
commonFarmerMistakes: Applying the entire N dose as basal instead of splitting it — huge leaching/volatilisation loss.
Broadcasting urea on dry, hot soil surface without irrigation — N escapes as ammonia gas.
Top-dressing urea just before or during heavy rain — it leaches straight down.
Applying N to waterlogged fields — lost through denitrification.
Using only DAP and assuming N is covered — DAP is mainly phosphorus.
Adding more and more urea when the crop looks pale, without checking if the real cause is waterlogging, cold, or poor roots.
Ignoring SULPHUR in oilseeds/pulses — crop shows N-like yellowing that urea won't fix.
Piling urea on legumes (soybean/moong/arhar/groundnut) — it suppresses natural nodulation.
Skipping Rhizobium seed treatment on legumes, then blaming soil N.
Over-N on paddy/wheat/sugarcane causing lodging and pest outbreaks.
Foliar urea stronger than 2% — scorches leaves.
Applying N with no soil moisture (dry soil) so it never reaches roots.
Not incorporating urea in alkaline/calcareous soils — maximum volatilisation.
Guessing dose by eye/habit instead of soil test or LCC.
Storing urea damp/open so it cakes and doses become inaccurate.
Mixing biofertiliser with chemical fertiliser/fungicide on seed at the same time, killing the microbes.
Late-season heavy N on wheat/mustard delaying maturity and inviting aphids.
Using canal/flood irrigation right after top-dress on sandy soil — flushes N away.
expertTips: Split N: roughly 1/2 to 1/3 basal and the rest at tillering/active-growth and pre-flowering.
Always irrigate lightly after urea top-dressing to move N into the root zone and cut ammonia loss.
In paddy/wheat, use the Leaf Colour Chart (LCC) — apply N only when leaf colour drops below the threshold.
For a fast rescue of visibly pale crop, spray 2% urea (20 g/L); greening shows in 4-7 days while soil N acts.
On sandy soils, go for more frequent, smaller doses rather than one large application.
On alkaline/calcareous soils, incorporate urea into soil — never leave it on the surface.
Use neem-coated urea (India's standard) for slower, more efficient release.
For legumes, treat seed with Rhizobium and give only a small STARTER N — let nodules do the rest.
Add sulphur (gypsum/ammonium sulphate/SSP) in oilseeds & pulses so N is actually used efficiently.
In fertigated vegetables/polyhouse, spoon-feed N via drip in small frequent doses for top efficiency.
Balance N with K at fruiting (e.g. KNO3) to avoid soft over-vegetative growth.
Diagnose before dosing: N deficiency starts on OLD leaves; if YOUNG leaves are yellow, it's a different nutrient (S, Fe, Zn).
Rule out waterlogging/cold/root disease before adding more N to a pale crop.
Use green manure (dhaincha/sunhemp) before paddy to add 20-30 kg N/acre biologically.
Keep FYM/compost going every season — it is the base that makes chemical N work better.
Time the last N dose early enough to avoid delayed maturity and lodging.
In maize, watch for the V-shaped tip-yellowing on lower leaves as the earliest reliable N signal.
Avoid N top-dress right before forecast heavy rain; wait for a drier window.
For sugarcane, apply N up to grand-growth only; late N lowers sugar recovery.
Keep a simple spray/fertiliser diary per plot so you can see what actually worked.

faq.q: Fasal pili ho gayi hai, kaun sa khaad daalein?
faq.a: Agar purani (neeche ki) pattiyan pehle pili ho rahi hain, to nitrogen ki kami hai — urea top-dress karein aur halki sinchai dein; jaldi hariyali ke liye 2% urea (20 g/L) ka chhidkav karein.
Soil test/crop schedule follow karein. Corrective top-dress ke roop me 25-45 kg urea/acre common hai, par poore season ka N 2-3 hisson me baantein.

faq.q: Urea daalne ka sahi tarika kya hai?
faq.a: Nam mitti par daalein aur turant halki sinchai karein ya mitti me mila dein — sukhi garam satah par daalne se gas ban kar ud jata hai.

faq.q: Kya urea aur DAP ek saath N de dete hain?
faq.a: DAP mukhya roop se phosphorus hai (18% N, 46% P). Sirf DAP par nirbhar na rahein — N ke liye urea top-dress zaroori hai.
2% se zyada nahi (20 g/L). Isse zyada karne par patti jal jati hai.

faq.q: Legume (soybean/moong/arhar) me zyada urea kyun mana hai?
faq.a: Zyada N nodules (jo khud N banate hain) ko dabata hai. Rhizobium se beej upchar karein aur sirf thoda starter N dein.

faq.q: Barish se pehle urea daal sakte hain?
faq.a: Nahi — tez barish N ko bahaकर le jati hai. Sukhe window ka intezar karein.

faq.q: Paani bhare khet me urea daalna theek hai?
faq.a: Nahi — waterlogging me N gas ban kar nikal jata hai (denitrification). Pehle paani nikaalein.

faq.q: Purani patti pili, nayi hari — kya matlab?
faq.a: Yeh classic nitrogen deficiency hai (N mobile hai, plant purani patti se N nayi patti me le jata hai).

faq.q: Nayi (upar ki) patti pili ho to?
faq.a: Tab N nahi — sulphur, iron ya zinc ki kami ho sakti hai. Sahi nutrient identify karein.

faq.q: Sabse efficient N application kaun sa hai?
faq.a: Drip fertigation me thodi-thodi baar dena, ya splits me soil top-dress + halki sinchai. Isse loss kam hota hai.
Yeh dheere N chhodta hai, use-efficiency badhta hai aur India me yahi standard subsidised urea hai.

faq.q: Pili fasal me aur urea daala par fayda nahi — kyun?
faq.a: Asli wajah waterlogging, thandi mitti, kharab jad, ya sulphur ki kami ho sakti hai. Pehle wajah pehchaanein.
Paddy/wheat me patti ka rang chart se milakar tay karte hain kab N dena hai — isse zaroorat par hi N padta hai, bachat hoti hai.

faq.q: FYM se N ki zaroorat khatam ho jati hai?
faq.a: Nahi, par FYM/compost mitti ki N-dene ki taakat badhata hai aur chemical N ko zyada asardar banata hai.
Fasal sirf patti-patti (over-green) ho jati hai, phool/phal kam, crop gir jati hai (lodging), keede-rog badhte hain, quality girti hai.

faq.q: Sugarcane me N kab tak dena?
faq.a: Grand-growth tak hi; der se N dene par cheeni recovery girti hai.

faq.q: Sandy (balui) mitti me N kaise dein?
faq.a: Chhoti-chhoti maatra me baar-baar dein, kyunki balui mitti se nitrate jaldi bah jata hai.

faq.q: Kya foliar urea soil urea ki jagah le sakta hai?
faq.a: Nahi — foliar sirf jaldi rescue/booster hai; poori N zaroorat soil/fertigation se hi poori hoti hai.

faq.q: CAN aur urea me kya farak?
faq.a: CAN (~25% N) me nitrate+calcium hota hai, thandi/geeli mitti aur acidic soil me fauran available; urea (46% N) sasta par volatilisation ka risk zyada.

faq.q: Cauliflower me chhoti curd/buttoning kya N se hai?
faq.a: N ki kami + galat variety/stress dono se hota hai. Early growth me N poora rakhein aur season ke hisaab se sahi variety chunein.
