TNAU Agritech Portal — nutrient management; ICAR-IIWBR, ICAR-IIRR crop-specific N schedules
Leaf Colour Chart (LCC) / SPAD-based N management research — ICAR & IRRI collaborative work
Soil Health Card scheme nutrient guidelines, Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, GoI
nutrients.name: Nitrogen
nutrients.mobility: Highly mobile in plant (deficiency shows in OLD leaves first) and highly mobile in soil (leaches easily)

nutrients.name: Phosphorus
nutrients.mobility: Mobile in plant (deficiency on OLD leaves first); very IMMOBILE in soil (fixed by Fe/Al in acid soils, Ca in alkaline soils)

overview.whyImportant: Phosphorus is the energy currency of the plant (ATP/ADP), drives root development, early establishment, flowering, seed/grain formation and crop maturity. Indian soils are widely P-deficient and P gets 'fixed' (locked) fast, so placement and timing matter more than for any other nutrient.

plantFunctions.roleInsidePlant: Core of ATP/ADP energy transfer, phospholipid membranes, and nucleic acids (DNA/RNA).
plantFunctions.photosynthesis: Supplies the ATP that powers carbon fixation and sugar transport.
plantFunctions.rootDevelopment: P's signature role — promotes vigorous, deep, branched roots and fast early establishment.
plantFunctions.vegetativeGrowth: Supports early seedling vigour and tillering; deficiency stunts the whole plant.
plantFunctions.flowering: Essential for flower initiation and timely, uniform flowering.
plantFunctions.fruitDevelopment: Drives fruit/pod/grain formation and hastens maturity.
plantFunctions.proteinSynthesis: Part of nucleic acids needed for protein synthesis and cell division.
plantFunctions.chlorophyllFormation: Indirect — supports energy for chlorophyll synthesis; deficiency gives dull dark/purple leaves.
plantFunctions.yieldContribution: Determines root vigour, grain/seed set and maturity; strong yield and quality response, especially in pulses/oilseeds.

whyDeficiencyHappens.why: P reacts with calcium to form insoluble calcium phosphates — locked away from roots.
P is fixed by iron and aluminium oxides into unavailable forms.
Low native P reserves and low organic matter.
Indirect — leaches bases, shifts pH and worsens fixation; erosion removes P-rich topsoil.
Poor root respiration cuts P uptake though availability may rise temporarily.
Cold wet soil slows P diffusion to roots.
Less organic P and fewer chelating acids that keep P available.
Very high P itself induces Zn/Fe deficiency; unbalanced N:P skews response.
Not a deficiency cause but induces Zn/Fe lock-up — relevant to over-DAP fields.
P is immobile in soil; roots must grow to it — shallow/damaged roots can't reach fixed P.
Low temperature sharply reduces P diffusion and uptake — classic early-Rabi purpling.
P moves to roots by diffusion in soil water; dry soil halts it.
Impaired root function lowers P absorption.
Broadcasting P on the surface (instead of band placement near roots) lets it fix before roots reach it.

symptoms.note: P is mobile in plant — deficiency starts on OLD/lower leaves. Signature sign is dull dark-green to PURPLE/reddish colour.
symptoms.early: Dull dark bluish-green leaves, slow growth, poor early vigour.
symptoms.middle: Purplish/reddish tints on older leaves, stems and leaf undersides (anthocyanin build-up); stunted plant, delayed flowering.
symptoms.severe: Older leaves bronze/purple then necrotic; severe stunting, very poor rooting, delayed maturity, drastically low seed/grain set.
symptoms.oldLeaves: First affected: dark dull green turning purple/red, especially undersides and margins.
symptoms.youngLeaves: Stay green; small and slow but rarely purple unless very severe.
symptoms.stem: Thin, short, often reddish-purple; reduced tillering/branching.
symptoms.root: Poor, sparse, shallow root system — a hallmark of P deficiency.
symptoms.seed: Few, poorly-filled seeds/grains; low seed quality.
symptoms.wholePlant: Stunted, dark-purplish, slow-maturing plant with weak roots and thin stand.

cropWiseSymptoms.symptoms: Dark dirty-green stunted seedlings, poor tillering, sometimes reddish older leaves; delayed panicle.
Dark green then purpling of older leaves, stunted plants, poor nodulation and pod set (P vital for legumes).
Dark stunted plants, poor pegging and pod fill; weak roots.
Dark dull leaves with purple tinge underside, stunted plants, delayed and poor flowering.
Slow dark-green plants, poor root/early vigour, delayed fruiting (in fertigation from low starter P).
Purplish older leaves, stunted plants, delayed small curds.
Dark purplish older leaves, slow head formation, small heads.
Dark dull vines, purple leaf undersides, poor root and fruit set.
Dark green to purplish older leaves, stunted plants, delayed flowering.
Dark stunted plants, reddish stems, delayed flowering and small pods.
Classic reddish-PURPLE margins/tips of lower leaves in young plants (esp. cold soil), stunting, poor cob fill.
Stunted dark plants, purplish tinge, poor tillering and earhead on P-poor sandy soils.
Dark green stunted plants, poor nodulation and pod set; purpling in cold spells.
Slow dark early growth, purpling, poor root and pod development.
Thin stunted canes, dull dark to purplish narrow leaves, poor tillering and root system.

howToFix.foliarSprayDose: 2% (20 g/L) at flowering for N+P boost
howToFix.bestCropStage: Basal at sowing/transplant (P must be available early)
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Band placement 5 cm below/beside seed — NOT surface broadcast
howToFix.precautions: Always band-place; surface P gets fixed before roots reach it. Avoid over-use (induces Zn deficiency).

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Band/broadcast + incorporate
howToFix.precautions: Bulky; ideal where S is also needed.

howToFix.bestCropStage: Early rooting & flower initiation in fertigated veg
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Drip fertigation or foliar
howToFix.precautions: High-P starter; keep balanced with K later.

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Band placement
howToFix.precautions: Balanced starter complexes; band-place.

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Broadcast + incorporate in acid soils only
howToFix.precautions: Only effective in acidic soils; useless in alkaline.

howToFix.soilApplicationDose: Seed treatment or 2-4 kg/acre with FYM
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Seed treatment / soil with FYM — unlocks fixed soil P
howToFix.precautions: Don't mix with fungicide on seed simultaneously.

methodOfApplication.broadcast: Least efficient for P (surface fixation) — only acceptable with immediate incorporation.
methodOfApplication.bandPlacement: BEST method for P — band 5 cm below/beside seed so roots reach it before it fixes.
methodOfApplication.spotApplication: Place near individual high-value plants (veg, sugarcane setts).
methodOfApplication.foliarSpray: 0.5-2% MAP/DAP for quick flower-stage correction; supplement only.
methodOfApplication.dripFertigation: Water-soluble MAP/12:61:00 at rooting stage; efficient for veg/polyhouse.
methodOfApplication.rootDrench: Dilute soluble-P at transplant for root establishment.
methodOfApplication.seedTreatment: PSB biofertiliser seed coat to solubilise soil P.

toxicity.whatHappens: P toxicity is rare and indirect — excess P INDUCES zinc and iron deficiency.
toxicity.plantToxicitySymptoms: Interveinal chlorosis of young leaves (Zn/Fe deficiency triggered by high P), not a direct P burn.
toxicity.yieldLoss: Through induced Zn/Fe hunger and wasted input cost.
toxicity.correctionMethods: Stop P application; apply Zn (ZnSO4) and Fe as needed; rebalance NPK; soil-test before next P dose.

prevention: Soil-test and apply P as per recommendation — P builds up and over-use is common.
Always BAND-PLACE P near the seed/root, never surface broadcast.
Apply full P as BASAL (P is immobile, top-dressing is ineffective).
Use SSP where sulphur is also needed (oilseeds/pulses).
Use PSB biofertiliser to unlock fixed soil P.
Correct extreme soil pH (lime acid soils, gypsum sodic soils) to reduce fixation.
Build organic matter to keep P available.
Avoid over-DAP that induces Zn/Fe deficiency.
In cold early-Rabi, expect temporary purpling — it eases as soil warms; ensure starter P placement.
Balance P with N, K and micronutrients.
commonFarmerMistakes: Broadcasting P on the surface — it fixes before roots reach it.
Top-dressing P mid-season expecting a response (P is immobile — must be basal).
Using rock phosphate on alkaline soils where it does nothing.
Over-applying DAP year after year, inducing zinc deficiency.
Ignoring soil test and applying blanket DAP.
Assuming purple maize/cold-soil purpling always needs more P (often just cold, self-corrects).
Not band-placing in cold Rabi soils where P uptake is already slow.
Skipping SSP in oilseeds/pulses and missing the sulphur they need.
Relying on DAP for both N and P but under-dosing N later.
Not using PSB to improve efficiency.
Applying P to waterlogged fields with poor root uptake.
Mixing PSB with fungicide on seed, killing the microbes.
Applying P without correcting soil pH, so most gets fixed.
Placing P too far from the seed row.
Believing more P means more yield — diminishing returns and Zn lock-up.
expertTips: Band-place P 5 cm below and beside the seed — the single biggest P-efficiency gain.
Give P as basal only; it won't move down from a top-dress.
Use SSP in soybean/groundnut/mustard for the bonus sulphur + calcium.
Add PSB biofertiliser to release the large pool of fixed soil P.
In cold early-Rabi, use a soluble starter P (12:61:00) for quick early uptake.
Don't panic at cold-soil purpling in maize; confirm with soil test before extra P.
Lime acidic soils to cut Fe/Al fixation and free up P.
Watch for Zn deficiency wherever heavy DAP has been used for years.
Keep FYM/compost going — organic acids keep P mobile.
Balance the N:P ratio; excess of either wastes the other.
For fertigated veg, front-load P at rooting, then shift to K at fruiting.
Test soil P (Olsen/Bray) every 2-3 years — many fields are already high.
Place PSB with FYM for best microbial survival.
Avoid P on waterlogged patches; fix drainage first.
Use complexes (12:32:16) as balanced basal starters.
Don't apply P and Zn in the same band — keep Zn separate to avoid reaction.
Ensure adequate soil moisture at basal P for diffusion to roots.
For pulses, P + Rhizobium together maximise nodulation and yield.
Track P build-up; step down doses on high-P fields to save cost.
Keep a plot-wise fertiliser diary to avoid repeat over-P.

faq.a: Basal me, beej ke 5 cm neeche/bagal me band-place karein. Surface par daalne se mitti me fix ho jata hai.
Nahi — P mitti me immobile hai, top-dress se jad tak nahi pahunchta. Hamesha basal dein.

faq.q: DAP aur SSP me kya farak?
faq.a: DAP me N+P zyada; SSP me P + sulphur + calcium. Oilseed/pulse me SSP behtar (sulphur milta hai).

faq.q: Maize me neeche ki patti purple kyun?
faq.a: P ki kami ya thandi mitti. Aksar thand se hota hai aur mitti garam hone par theek ho jata hai; soil test se confirm karein.

faq.q: Rock phosphate kab kaam karta hai?
faq.a: Sirf acidic (kam pH) mitti me; alkaline mitti me bekaar.
Phosphate Solubilising Bacteria — mitti me fix P ko ghol kar available banata hai. Beej upchar ya FYM ke saath dein.
Zyada P zinc aur iron ki kami paida karta hai (nayi patti pili). Soil test ke bina blanket DAP na dein.
Purani patti dull dark-green se PURPLE/reddish, paudha bauna, jad kamzor.
Shuruaat me (rooting/early growth) sabse zaroori — isliye basal placement.
Haan, flowering par 0.5-2% MAP/DAP quick correction ke liye, par yeh sirf supplement hai.

faq.q: Alkaline mitti me P kyun lock hota hai?
faq.a: Calcium ke saath insoluble calcium phosphate ban jata hai. pH sudhaar + band placement madad karta hai.
P + Rhizobium se nodulation aur pod set badhta hai; oilseed/pulse P ke liye bahut responsive hain.

faq.q: Cold soil me P kaise dein?
faq.a: Soluble starter P (12:61:00) band/fertigation me, taaki thandi mitti me bhi jaldi mile.

faq.q: P ka asar kitne din me?
faq.a: P dheera hai — 10-20 din; foliar 7-12 din.

faq.q: Kya P har saal utna hi dena?
faq.a: Nahi — P mitti me jama hota hai. High-P kheton me dose kam karein, soil test dekhein.

faq.q: P ke saath Zn mila kar de sakte hain?
faq.a: Ek hi band me nahi — P aur Zn alag dein, warna reaction se Zn lock ho jata hai.
Pehle drainage theek karein; geeli mitti me jad P nahi le pati.
Lagbhag 11% S — isliye oilseed/pulse me double fayda.
Achha balanced basal starter; band-place karein.

faq.q: P zyada dene se yield badhegi?
faq.a: Ek limit ke baad nahi — fazool kharch aur Zn/Fe ki kami. Soil test ke hisab se dein.

references: ICAR Handbook of Agriculture; ICAR-IISS Bhopal
IARI New Delhi nutrient guidelines
SAU Package of Practices (PAU, GBPUAT, ANGRAU, UAS)
KVK advisories; TNAU Agritech Portal
FCO product specifications; Soil Health Card scheme, GoI

nutrients.name: Potassium
nutrients.mobility: Mobile in plant (deficiency on OLD leaves first, classic MARGINAL leaf scorch); variable in soil

overview.whyImportant: Potassium is the 'quality and stress' nutrient — it regulates water (stomata), builds stem strength (anti-lodging), improves disease/drought/cold tolerance, and drives sugar/starch transport that determines fruit size, colour, TSS, and grain filling. It is not built into tissue like N/P but activates 60+ enzymes and governs plant health.

plantFunctions.roleInsidePlant: Osmotic regulation, stomatal control, enzyme activation, sugar/starch translocation, protein synthesis regulation.
plantFunctions.photosynthesis: Regulates stomatal opening (CO2 intake) and moves photosynthates (sugars) from leaves to sink organs.
plantFunctions.rootDevelopment: Supports root growth and drought resilience via better water regulation.
plantFunctions.vegetativeGrowth: Maintains turgor and vigour; deficiency weakens tissue.
plantFunctions.flowering: Supports flower/fruit retention and reduces drop.
plantFunctions.fruitDevelopment: KEY role — fills fruit/grain, improves size, colour, sugar/TSS, shelf-life and grain weight.
plantFunctions.proteinSynthesis: Regulates enzymes of protein and starch synthesis.
plantFunctions.chlorophyllFormation: Indirect — healthy K keeps leaves green and functional; deficiency scorches margins.
plantFunctions.yieldContribution: Determines grain/fruit filling, test weight, quality and stress survival; huge in fruit/tuber/sugar crops.

whyDeficiencyHappens.why: Ca/Mg dominance on exchange sites can suppress K uptake (antagonism).
Leaching of K and Al toxicity impair root uptake.
Low K-holding capacity; K+ leaches readily.
K+ leaches from light soils.
Poor root respiration/function reduces active K uptake.
Flushes K below root zone on light soils.
Less K retention and cycling.
Excess Mg or Ca or ammonium antagonises K uptake.
Unbalanced high P/N with low K starves the quality-driving nutrient.
Cationic competition blocks K entry into roots.
Restricted roots can't forage enough K.
Slows K diffusion and root uptake.
K reaches roots by diffusion in soil water; dry soil stalls it.
High Na competes with and blocks K uptake.
Under-application (farmers over-focus on N/P), and clay-fixation of K in heavy soils.

symptoms.note: K is mobile — deficiency starts on OLD/lower leaves. Signature: MARGINAL yellowing then brown SCORCHING of leaf edges/tips.
symptoms.early: Older leaves show yellowing at tips and margins; slightly weak growth.
symptoms.middle: Marginal chlorosis turns to brown 'burnt' scorched edges on older leaves; interveinal yellowing; weak stems.
symptoms.severe: Older leaves scorched/necrotic from margins inward, lodging, poor small fruit/grain, higher pest & disease incidence.
symptoms.oldLeaves: First and worst: yellow then brown scorched margins/tips while midrib stays green (classic 'leaf-edge burn').
symptoms.root: Reduced root vigour and drought tolerance.
symptoms.fruit: Small, poorly coloured, low-sugar/TSS fruit; uneven ripening; poor shelf-life; poor grain filling.
symptoms.wholePlant: Weak lodging-prone plant with scorched lower leaves, poor-quality small produce and low stress tolerance.

cropWiseSymptoms.symptoms: Older-leaf tip/margin yellowing to brown scorch, weak lodging-prone stems, poor grain filling, higher brown-spot incidence.
Marginal yellow-brown scorch on older leaves, poor pod fill, small seeds.
Older-leaf margin scorch, poor pod fill and kernel weight.
Older-leaf edge scorch, flower/fruit drop, small dull fruit, poor drought tolerance.
Leaf-margin scorch, poor fruit size/colour/wall thickness, blossom-end problems under stress.
Older-leaf marginal scorch, small poor curds, weak plants.
Marginal leaf scorch, small loose heads, poor stress tolerance.
Older-leaf edge scorch, mis-shapen fruit (narrow stem-end), low fruit quality.
Older-leaf margin browning, small dull fruit, flower drop.
Older-leaf edge scorch, small fibrous pods, weak plants.
Classic yellow-brown scorching of lower-leaf MARGINS, weak stalks that lodge, poor tip-fill / loose cobs.
Older-leaf marginal scorch, lodging, poor grain fill on light soils.
Older-leaf edge yellow-brown scorch, poor pod fill.
Marginal older-leaf scorch, poor pod fill, weak stems.
Older-leaf margin/tip scorching (firing), weak lodging-prone canes, LOW SUGAR recovery — K is critical for sucrose.

howToFix.foliarSprayDose: Not preferred (chloride); use SOP/KNO3 for foliar
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Soil band/broadcast + incorporate; split on light soils
howToFix.precautions: Chloride — avoid on chloride-sensitive crops (tobacco, some fruit) and saline soils; use SOP instead.

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Soil, fertigation or foliar
howToFix.precautions: Costlier; best for quality fruit/veg and where chloride is a concern; bonus sulphur.

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Fertigation or foliar — supplies nitrate-N + K together
howToFix.precautions: Don't tank-mix with calcium products.

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Fertigation or foliar
howToFix.precautions: Ideal quality-stage K without extra N.

howToFix.fertilizer: Organic: FYM / crop residue / wood ash
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Broadcast + incorporate; residue return recycles K
howToFix.precautions: Retaining crop residue (esp. paddy straw) recycles large K — don't burn it.

methodOfApplication.broadcast: Common for MOP basal + top-dress; incorporate into moist soil.
methodOfApplication.bandPlacement: Efficient basal K placement near root zone.
methodOfApplication.foliarSpray: SOP/KNO3 (0.5-1%) for quick fruiting-stage quality boost and stress relief.
methodOfApplication.dripFertigation: Spoon-feed soluble K (KNO3/SOP) through fruiting — highest efficiency, standard for veg/polyhouse/sugarcane drip.
methodOfApplication.rootDrench: Dilute K at root zone under stress.

toxicity.whatHappens: Direct K toxicity is rare; excess K causes CATIONIC ANTAGONISM — induced Mg (and Ca) deficiency.
toxicity.plantToxicitySymptoms: Interveinal yellowing of older leaves (induced Mg deficiency); in extremes, salt effect from high MOP.
toxicity.yieldLoss: Via induced Mg/Ca deficiency and wasted cost; salinity stress on light soils.
toxicity.qualityReduction: Mg-deficiency chlorosis lowers photosynthesis and quality.
toxicity.correctionMethods: Stop K; apply MgSO4 (soil/foliar) to correct induced Mg deficiency; use SOP not MOP on saline soils; rebalance based on soil test.

prevention: Soil-test and apply K per recommendation — it's the most under-applied major nutrient in India.
Split K (basal + fruiting) on light/sandy soils to cut leaching.
Return crop residue (don't burn straw) to recycle large amounts of K.
Use SOP for quality fruit/veg and chloride-sensitive or saline situations.
Feed K especially at flowering-fruiting for size, colour, and grain fill.
Balance K with Mg and Ca to avoid antagonism.
Build organic matter for K retention.
On heavy clay soils, account for K-fixation and apply adequately.
Use fertigation to spoon-feed K in high-value crops.
Ensure moisture at K application for diffusion to roots.
commonFarmerMistakes: Focusing only on N and P and skipping K entirely — the commonest mistake.
Burning crop residue/paddy straw, wasting recycled K.
Applying all K basal on sandy soils where it leaches — should split.
Using MOP on chloride-sensitive crops or saline soils instead of SOP.
Over-applying MOP and inducing magnesium deficiency.
Ignoring K at the fruiting stage, then getting small poor-quality fruit.
Assuming leaf-edge scorch is a disease and spraying fungicide.
Not splitting K on light soils.
Blaming lodging on wind alone — low K weakens stems.
Skipping soil test and guessing K need.
Applying K to dry soil so it doesn't reach roots.
Over-liming/over-Mg causing K antagonism.
Using cheap high-N blends with no K in fruit crops.
Not correcting induced Mg deficiency after heavy K.
Believing K isn't needed because the crop 'looks green' (quality loss is invisible until harvest).
expertTips: Prioritise K at flowering-fruiting — it's the quality/size/colour/sugar nutrient.
Return crop residue; retained paddy/wheat straw recycles substantial K.
Split K on sandy soils: part basal, part at fruiting.
Use SOP (not MOP) for tomato/chilli/capsicum/fruit and saline soils — better quality, no chloride.
KNO3 foliar (1%) at fruiting doubles as a stress-reliever (heat/drought).
Diagnose K by the classic OLD-leaf MARGINAL scorch with green midrib.
Watch stems — low K = weak, lodging-prone crop; K firms tissue.
After heavy MOP, add MgSO4 to prevent induced Mg deficiency.
In sugarcane, adequate K is essential for sucrose recovery, not just tonnage.
Feed K through drip in small frequent doses for top efficiency.
Balance the K:Mg:Ca ratio; excess of one blocks the others.
Ensure soil moisture at K application (diffusion-dependent).
On heavy clay, apply enough K to satisfy fixation before the crop gets its share.
For grain crops, late K supports test-weight and filling.
Use 00:00:50 for a clean quality-stage K push without extra N.
Don't tank-mix KNO3 with calcium products (precipitate).
Match K rate to yield target — high-yield fruit/tuber crops remove a lot of K.
Test soil K every 2-3 years; many fields have declined from residue burning.
Combine K with balanced N for filling without over-vegetative growth.
Keep a plot diary of K applications vs fruit quality to fine-tune.

faq.q: Patti ke kinare jal (brown) rahe hain, kya karein?
faq.a: Agar purani patti ke kinare pehle pile/brown ho rahe hain aur midrib hari hai, to potassium ki kami hai — MOP/SOP soil me dein, fruiting par KNO3/SOP 1% chhidkav karein.

faq.q: MOP aur SOP me kya farak?
faq.a: MOP (60% K) sasta par chloride wala; SOP (50% K + sulphur) mehnga par quality fruit/veg aur saline/chloride-sensitive ke liye behtar.
Flowering se fruiting tak — size, rang, meethas (TSS), grain-fill aur stress tolerance ke liye.
Nahi — straw me bahut K hota hai. Jalane se K barbaad; residue mitti me milayein.

faq.q: Sandy mitti me K kaise dein?
faq.a: Split me — kuch basal, kuch fruiting par — kyunki balui mitti se K bah jata hai.
Magnesium ki kami induce ho jati hai (purani patti interveinal pili). MgSO4 se sudhaar karein.
K se sucrose (cheeni) recovery badhti hai aur cane girna (lodging) kam hota hai — sirf tonnage nahi, quality bhi.
K deficiency purani patti ke kinare se shuru hoti hai aur symmetric hoti hai; rog aksar spot/patchy. Confirm karke hi spray karein.
KNO3 (1%) ya SOP (0.5%) — MOP foliar na karein (chloride).

faq.q: Lodging (fasal girna) K se juda hai?
faq.a: Haan — K stem strong karta hai. Kam K = kamzor tana, girne ka khatra.
SOP behtar — quality, rang aur bina chloride ke; saath me sulphur bhi.
KNO3/SOP thodi-thodi baar drip se fruiting tak — sabse efficient.

faq.q: K ka asar kitne din me?
faq.a: Foliar 5-10 din, soil 7-15 din (nami ke saath).
SOP use karein, MOP nahi — kyunki chloride aur salt load badha deta hai.

faq.q: Kya K se fruit meetha hota hai?
faq.a: Haan — K sugar transport chalaता hai, isliye TSS/meethas aur rang badhta hai.

faq.q: Clay (bhaari) mitti me K fix hota hai?
faq.a: Haan, kuch K clay me fix ho jata hai — isliye paryapt maatra dein.

faq.q: Sirf green dikhne par K chhod dein?
faq.a: Nahi — K ki kami quality me chhupi rehti hai; harvest par chhoti kamzor fasal se pata chalta hai.

faq.q: K ke saath Mg dena chahiye?
faq.a: Haan, balance rakhein — zyada K, Mg uptake rok deta hai.

faq.q: Wood ash se K milta hai?
faq.a: Haan, lakdi ki raakh me 5-8% K2O; thodi maatra me soil me use kar sakte hain.
Soil test + yield target se — zyada upaj wali fruit/tuber fasal bahut K nikalti hain.

references: ICAR Handbook of Agriculture; ICAR-IISS Bhopal
IARI New Delhi; SAU Package of Practices
KVK advisories; TNAU Agritech Portal
Potash Research Institute of India; FCO specs
Soil Health Card scheme, GoI

nutrients.name: Silicon
nutrients.mobility: Immobile once deposited (deposited in older tissue; new growth weak under deficiency)

overview.whyImportant: Silicon is a beneficial (near-essential for rice, sugarcane, other grasses) nutrient that strengthens cell walls, making plants stand erect (anti-lodging), resist pests/diseases (blast, stem borer, BPH) and tolerate drought/salinity. Rice and sugarcane remove huge amounts of Si.

plantFunctions.roleInsidePlant: Deposited as silica in cell walls/leaf surface — mechanical strength + protective barrier.
plantFunctions.photosynthesis: Keeps leaves erect for better light capture; reduces mutual shading.
plantFunctions.rootDevelopment: Improves root oxidation power (esp. in flooded rice).
plantFunctions.fruitDevelopment: Better grain filling from erect efficient canopy.
plantFunctions.enzymeActivation: Not an enzyme cofactor; acts structurally/defensively.
plantFunctions.yieldContribution: Large in rice/sugarcane — lodging resistance, pest/disease tolerance, grain/cane yield.

whyDeficiencyHappens.why: Old red/laterite soils are depleted of plant-available Si.
Low Si reserves.
These crops remove large Si; residue removal/burning drains it.
Leaches soluble silicic acid.
Naturally low mineral Si.
Rice straw is Si-rich; burning/removing it depletes soil Si.
Less Si cycling.
Alters silicic acid availability.
Not returning residue; ignoring Si in intensive rice/cane.

symptoms.note: Deficiency = weak, soft, droopy plants prone to lodging and pests; less a colour symptom, more a structural/health one.
symptoms.early: Soft, less-erect leaves; slightly higher pest attack.
symptoms.middle: Drooping leaves, weak stems/tillers, more lodging and pest/disease (blast, borer, BPH) pressure.
symptoms.severe: Severe lodging, high disease/pest incidence, reduced grain/cane yield.
symptoms.oldLeaves: Soft, may show more disease lesions.
symptoms.root: Reduced oxidising power in flooded rice.
symptoms.fruit: Poor grain filling from lodged/shaded canopy.
symptoms.wholePlant: Soft, droopy, lodging-prone, pest/disease-susceptible — the classic low-Si rice/cane picture.

cropWiseSymptoms.symptoms: Droopy soft leaves, lodging, higher blast/brown-spot/stem-borer/BPH incidence, poor grain fill — rice is the top Si-responsive crop.
Minor — some improvement in stress tolerance with Si (not a major responder).
Minor Si response.
Si can improve disease/stress tolerance (secondary).
Si aids stress/disease tolerance in protected culture (secondary).
Minor Si response.
Minor Si response.
Si notably reduces powdery mildew and improves vine strength — useful in cucurbits.
Minor Si response.
Minor Si response.
Stronger stalks, some lodging/pest tolerance with Si.
Stronger stems on Si-poor sandy soils.
Minor Si response.
Minor Si response.
Weak lodging-prone canes, higher borer/disease, lower sugar on Si-depleted soils — major Si responder.

howToFix.soilApplicationDose: 100-200 kg/acre basal on Si-deficient rice/cane soils
howToFix.methodOfApplication: Broadcast + incorporate; supplies Si + raises pH on acid soils
howToFix.precautions: Also acts as liming material — watch pH on already-alkaline soils.

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Foliar/fertigation — quick Si + K, aids mildew resistance
howToFix.precautions: High pH solution — don't tank-mix with acidic products or many pesticides.

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Incorporate rice straw instead of burning — recycles Si (and K)
howToFix.precautions: Do NOT burn residue — the cheapest Si (and K) recycling.

howToFix.methodOfApplication: Soil/foliar amendment source of plant-available Si
howToFix.precautions: Use FCO/quality products.

methodOfApplication.broadcast: Calcium silicate/slag broadcast + incorporate on rice/cane soils.
methodOfApplication.foliarSpray: Potassium silicate foliar for quick Si + mildew suppression in veg/cucurbits.
methodOfApplication.seedTreatment: Not typical; residue return is the main practical route.

toxicity.whatHappens: Si toxicity is essentially not a field problem; excess silicate can raise pH.
toxicity.yieldLoss: Only indirect via pH shift from over-liming silicate.
toxicity.correctionMethods: Manage pH if over-applying silicate liming material.

prevention: Incorporate crop residue (don't burn) — the main Si (and K) recycling.
Apply calcium silicate on Si-depleted rice/cane soils.
Use potassium silicate foliar in disease-prone veg/cucurbits.
Build organic matter.
Avoid continuous Si-removal without replenishment in rice-cane systems.
Balance pH when using silicate liming.
Return straw to paddy fields.
Use quality FCO silicon products.
Prioritise Si in high-yield rice/sugarcane.

nutrients.name: Sulphur
nutrients.mobility: IMMOBILE / poorly mobile (deficiency on YOUNG leaves first — looks like N but on new growth)

overview.whyImportant: Sulphur is the 4th major nutrient and the #1 hidden deficiency in Indian oilseeds and pulses. It builds sulphur-containing amino acids (cysteine, methionine), proteins, oils and the enzymes of N metabolism. A crop can look N-deficient but actually be S-starved — urea won't fix it.

plantFunctions.roleInsidePlant: Component of amino acids cysteine/methionine, proteins, coenzyme A, vitamins, and oil synthesis.
plantFunctions.rootDevelopment: Supports nodulation in legumes and root growth.
plantFunctions.vegetativeGrowth: Needed for protein-building; deficiency yellows new growth.
plantFunctions.fruitDevelopment: Drives OIL content in oilseeds and pungency in alliums/crucifers.
plantFunctions.proteinSynthesis: Essential — S-amino acids are the backbone of proteins.
plantFunctions.enzymeActivation: Part of key enzymes including those of N assimilation.
plantFunctions.chlorophyllFormation: Indirect but strong — deficiency causes chlorosis.
plantFunctions.yieldContribution: Major in oilseeds/pulses/crucifers — oil %, protein, and yield.

whyDeficiencyHappens.why: Sandy alkaline soils often low in S; leaching of sulphate.
Acid sandy soils low in organic S.
Low organic matter (main S source) and sulphate leaches.
Sulphate is mobile and leaches like nitrate.
Sulphate reduced to sulphide, unavailable/toxic in anaerobic soil.
Leaches sulphate.
Most soil S comes from organic matter mineralisation — low OM = low S.
High N without S limits protein synthesis; N:S ratio matters.
Shift from ammonium sulphate/SSP to urea/DAP removed the incidental S farmers used to add — the main modern cause.
Less foraging for sulphate.
Slows organic-S mineralisation.
Slows mineralisation and uptake.
Impaired uptake.
Ignoring S entirely in oilseed/pulse nutrition — extremely common.

symptoms.note: S is poorly mobile — deficiency starts on YOUNG/upper leaves (unlike N which hits old leaves). Uniform pale-yellow young growth.
symptoms.early: Young/upper leaves pale-green to yellow, uniform (whole leaf, not interveinal).
symptoms.middle: General yellowing of newer leaves, stunted spindly growth, delayed maturity.
symptoms.severe: Whole plant pale-yellow, stunted, thin stems, poor pods/oil; older leaves may also yellow later.
symptoms.youngLeaves: First and worst: uniform pale-yellow (chlorosis of the whole young leaf).
symptoms.stem: Thin, short, woody; reddish tinge in some crops.
symptoms.fruit: Low oil content in oilseeds, poor pod fill.
symptoms.seed: Low oil %, reduced protein and seed quality.
symptoms.wholePlant: Pale-yellow especially at the top, stunted, late-maturing — the 'N-deficiency on the wrong (young) leaves' picture.

cropWiseSymptoms.symptoms: Young leaves pale-yellow, stunted, delayed — on sandy/leached soils.
Upper leaves uniformly pale-yellow, poor nodulation, low oil — S is critical for soybean.
Young-leaf yellowing, poor nodulation and low oil; responds strongly to gypsum.
Young leaves pale, stunted plants, poor growth.
Pale new leaves in low-S fertigation.
Young-leaf yellowing, whip-like thin leaves in severe cases (crucifers are high S-users).
Pale young leaves, reduced head size (high S demand crop).
Pale upper leaves, weak vines.
Young-leaf yellowing, stunted growth.
Pale new leaves, thin plants.

Pale young leaves on sandy soils.
Young-leaf yellowing, poor nodulation and pod fill.
Pale new growth, poor nodulation.

toxicity.whatHappens: S toxicity is rare; excess elemental S sharply LOWERS soil pH; in waterlogged soil sulphate becomes toxic H2S/sulphide.

toxicity.yieldLoss: Via acidity or sulphide root damage.
toxicity.correctionMethods: Avoid over-use of elemental S; improve drainage/aeration in rice to prevent sulphide; lime if over-acidified.

nutrients.name: Zinc
nutrients.mobility: Partially mobile (deficiency mostly on YOUNGER-to-MIDDLE leaves; interveinal + short internodes)

overview.whyImportant: Zinc is the MOST widespread micronutrient deficiency in Indian soils (esp. rice-wheat belt). It is vital for auxin (growth hormone) synthesis, enzyme systems and protein metabolism. Deficiency causes stunting, short internodes, and interveinal chlorosis — 'khaira' in rice.

plantFunctions.roleInsidePlant: Enzyme cofactor (carbonic anhydrase, dehydrogenases), auxin (IAA) synthesis, protein metabolism.
plantFunctions.vegetativeGrowth: Drives internode elongation — deficiency gives rosetting/short internodes.
plantFunctions.proteinSynthesis: Needed for ribosome integrity and protein synthesis.
plantFunctions.yieldContribution: Large in rice/wheat/maize/citrus; deficiency sharply cuts yield.

whyDeficiencyHappens.why: Zn forms insoluble compounds above pH 7 — main cause.

symptoms.early: Middle/young leaves interveinal yellowing; slightly reduced growth.

symptoms.wholePlant: Stunted rosetted plant with interveinal chlorosis/bronzing and short internodes — the khaira/little-leaf picture.
